title: LLMs are stuck in a groupthink groove. This startup is trying to get them out.
gist: Mainstream LLMs converge on near-identical, predictable answers to open-ended prompts; Australian startup Springboards built Flint (on Alibaba's Qwen 3) to selectively inject variety at key decision points, targeting advertising/marketing creatives as a wedge market.

themes.theme: LLM homogeneity as a structural flaw

insights.claim: Large language models are far more predictable and less creative than users expect—asking for a random number 1–10 almost always returns 7, and asking for a car name yields Toyota or Honda—because they converge on high-probability responses.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Output homogeneity is a concrete product gap creating an opening for differentiation-focused AI tooling.
insights.quote: Most language models are fighting hallucinations. We welcome them.
insights.locator: Most language models are fighting hallucinations

insights.claim: A NeurIPS best-paper study titled 'Artificial Hivemind' found 25 different LLMs (US, Chinese, and open-source) converged on nearly identical answers to open-ended prompts, with most metaphors-about-time collapsing to 'Time is a river' or 'Time is a weaver' across 1,250 responses.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Cross-model convergence is now peer-validated, suggesting a persistent industry-wide limitation, not a single-vendor bug.
insights.locator: In November a team of researchers put out a paper

insights.claim: Different LLMs likely converge because most are trained in similar ways on similar data to do similar tasks, implying homogeneity stems from shared training paradigms rather than any one architecture.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: If homogeneity is rooted in shared data/methods, novelty becomes a defensible moat for firms that diverge on training.
insights.locator: It’s not clear exactly why this happens

insights.claim: Springboards built Flint on top of Alibaba's open-source Qwen 3 rather than training a foundation model, explicitly because foundation-model training is too expensive for a small team.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Illustrates open-weight Chinese models as the cost-effective base layer for niche Western AI startups.
insights.quote: Training a foundation model is not on the table for us. It’s just too expensive.
insights.locator: Springboards built Flint on top of Qwen 3

insights.claim: Simply raising the temperature parameter fails as a creativity lever because it makes models incoherent—OpenAI's model at max temperature switched from English into code mid-sentence—so Flint instead trains the model to boost randomness only at specific output points where variety is possible.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Selective, position-aware randomness is the technical insight; a reusable approach for controllable creative generation.
insights.quote: It does not make sense to dial up the randomness across the board; you only want to boost it at specific points in its output
insights.locator: Most LLMs have settings that let you adjust

insights.claim: OpenAI concedes that training models for reliable, coherent answers pushes them toward familiar high-probability responses, and that harder pushes for novelty tend to produce weaker or less reliable output.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Frames a genuine reliability-vs-novelty tradeoff that constrains how far incumbents will chase creativity.

insights.claim: Flint is a still-buggy prototype aimed narrowly at advertisers and marketers—Springboards's existing customers—and 'falls over' when pushed too far, positioning it as a supplementary brainstorming tool alongside ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini rather than a replacement.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Early, fragile product with a narrow beachhead—commercial scalability beyond creative niches is unproven.
insights.quote: It sometimes falls over when you start pushing it too far
insights.locator: She notes that Flint is still a prototype

insights.claim: A marketing-firm user notes that nine times out of ten the 'average' LLM answer is fine because most users want mass-market familiar output, implying demand for high-novelty models is inherently limited.
insights.speaker: Will Douglas Heaven
insights.so_what: Caps the total addressable market for creativity-first models—novelty is a feature, not a mass need.
insights.quote: Most people are fine with good enough. They want to see mass-market familiar things.
insights.locator: And yet Weigl notes that nine times out of 10
